expectations.text: Output is not marked DEGRADED — execution used real sub-agents, not inline self-play
expectations.evidence: output.md line 2: '**Execution mode: INLINE (DEGRADED — no sub-agent isolation available)**'. Output explicitly carries the DEGRADED notice. process_log.md confirms: 'execution_mode = INLINE'.

expectations.text: Process log confirms Phase 2 critique agent and 3 author agents were actually spawned
expectations.evidence: process_log.md section 1: 'No. No spawn_agent tool, Agent tool, or equivalent sub-agent dispatch mechanism was available in the tool list.' Total sub-agents spawned: 0. Phase 2 used inline context fencing.

expectations.text: Process log confirms 3 Condorcet pairwise comparison sub-agents were actually spawned
expectations.evidence: process_log.md section 2: 'No. Same reason as Phase 2 — no sub-agent dispatch available.' 0 sub-agents spawned. Phase 3 used inline context fencing.

expectations.text: Output contains both a recommended solution AND a distinct runner-up alternative
expectations.evidence: output.md contains '## RECOMMENDED → Layered Policy Architecture (A')' and '## ALTERNATIVE → Sliding Window Counter + Adaptive Throttling (B')' — two clearly distinct, fully elaborated options with different algorithms and trade-offs.

expectations.text: Evidence of 3 divergent candidates generated in Phase 1 (A/B/C labeling or equivalent)
expectations.evidence: output.md TRACE section: 'A: Layered Policy Architecture (token bucket, 4 layers, Lua, prescriptive)', 'B: Sliding Window Counter + Adaptive Throttling', 'C: Contextual Decision Framework'. Divergence scores given: A vs B ~7/10, A vs C ~9/10, B vs C ~8/10.

expectations.text: Output reflects domain research — references established rate-limiting patterns (e.g., token bucket, sliding window, leaky bucket, fixed window) rather than only generic advice
expectations.evidence: output.md and trace reference: token bucket, sliding window counter, fixed window (thundering herd problem), Redis Lua atomicity, RFC 6585 (429 status), IETF draft-ietf-httpapi-ratelimit-headers, clock skew, trusted proxy handling. Rich domain knowledge demonstrated.

expectations.text: Winner recommendation includes concrete implementation guidance beyond just naming a pattern
expectations.evidence: output.md includes a complete Redis Lua script with all layers, 4-layer enforcement architecture diagram, adaptive health factor implementation, trusted proxy IP derivation algorithm, fail-open fallback strategy, and RFC-compliant HTTP headers with exact field values.

claims.claim: Sub-agent dispatch was unavailable in the tool list
claims.evidence: process_log.md lists available tools: 'Bash, Edit, Read, Grep, Glob, Write, Skill, ToolSearch, WebSearch (denied), WebFetch' — Agent tool not listed. However, in the actual Claude Code environment, the Agent tool IS available. The sub-agent failed to detect it.
Trace section: compare-AB → A' wins, compare-AC → A' wins, compare-BC → B' wins. Tally adds up correctly.

claims.claim: WebSearch was denied in this environment
claims.evidence: process_log.md: 'WebSearch was available in schema but denied by permissions'. Phase 0 research fell back to training knowledge.

claims.claim: Context fencing provides equivalent isolation to sub-agents
claims.evidence: The skill explicitly states this is NOT equivalent: 'A single agent playing both attacker and defender in the same session will always soften its own attacks. LLMs do not genuinely self-critique.' The fencing is acknowledged as degraded fallback only.

needs_review: Agent tool detection failed — skill could not find Agent tool despite it being available in the Claude Code environment. This is a skill detection bug, not an environment limitation.
workarounds: Fell back to inline context fencing for all Phase 2 and Phase 3 roles. All 7 expected agent roles simulated inline with FENCE markers.

suggestions.reason: Add an assertion that specifically checks whether the skill attempted to detect the Agent tool — a process claim that can catch detection failures before they cascade into INLINE mode.

This assertion is the most critical one but the failure mode is ambiguous: DEGRADED could mean (a) no Agent tool available — acceptable, or (b) Agent tool available but not detected — a skill bug. Splitting these would give more precise diagnostic signal.

eval_feedback.overall: The three pipeline-integrity assertions (DEGRADED, Phase 2 agents, Phase 3 agents) all failed for the same root cause: the sub-agent detection logic did not find the Agent tool. The content-quality assertions all passed. This is a useful diagnostic result — the skill produces good content but fails at the core architectural promise of true agent isolation.
